title: Letters to the Editor
summary: Washington state angler Scott Ames takes advantage of shirt-sleeve weather and flat seas off Deep Creek to haul up another halibut on May 2. Shirley Ann skipper Mike Chihuly of Ninilchik spent much of the morning releasing 10- to 20-pound halibut as anglers kept looking for a bigger catch. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Suspected SARS case improving ANCHORAGE: Pilot's fever is gone, but he is still isolated pending test results. By ANN POTEMPA Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 24, 2003) The first Alaska patient with suspected SARS is recovering at Providence Alaska Medical Center in Anchorage, said Dr. Bruce Chandler, the city's medical officer. One week after arriving in Anchorage, the patient's temperature had returned to normal, Chandler said Friday. The patient arrived at Providence on Sunday morning with a 104.9 degree temperature, said Dr. Beth Funk, medical epidemiologist. Little information was available about the patient when he was first hospitalized, but Funk shared more at a seminar Friday organized by the American Lung Association of Alaska. The man is the 28-year-old co-pilot of an ai…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Experimental septic system works in wet soil Barbara and Clair Ramsey Housing (Published: May 25, 2003) Q. I hope to someday build a house. However, some of the available lots are wet because of a high water table, and I don't want to use a holding tank for a septic system. Are there any alternatives? A. Many lots seem unlikely to be developed because the water table is too high to allow a conventional septic system. The good news is that this is about to change. By the end of this summer, when the final test period is over for the first installed system, the Municipality of Anchorage should start permitting a new, innovative system for these problem lots. Aptly named The NoMound, this innovative system is the high-tech alternative to the unsightly, elevated mounds commonly used when s…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Ready to respond Village firefighters prepare for wildfire season By JOEL GAY Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 25, 2003) Jim Nikolai leads inbound runners as walkers continue the other way during firefighter fitness testing last week in Nikolai. Before qualifying to fight forest fires, individuals must either run 1.5 miles without a pack in 11 minutes and 40 seconds, or walk three miles in 45 minutes with 45 pounds of water on their backs. (Photo by Erik Hill / Anchorage Daily News) Mary Ellen Kimball of Nikolai, center, and forestry technician Elsie Norback of McGrath cheer firefighters as they reach the halfway point of the three-mile pack test last week in Nikolai. The state Division of Forestry is conducting refresher courses for the Southwest Region's 73 village emergency fire…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Native corporation invests in business that shares autos FLEXCAR: $1 million will update fleet of cars that start with password. By PAULA DOBBYN Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 8, 2003) Bristol Bay Native Corp. has invested $1 million in Seattle-based Flexcar, a car-sharing service. The Native firm, headquartered in Anchorage, will invest another $1 million provided Flexcar meets certain benchmarks, said Steve Tolton, chief financial officer. Flexcar offers its members access to a fleet of roughly 230 vehicles in Seattle, Portland, Ore., Los Angeles, San Diego and the Washington, D.C., area. Members pay a one-time fee of $25. Afterward, they pay $7 an hour any time they use a car and get 10 free miles. Most people use Flexcars from two to three hours and drive them less than eight…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Community Profile (Published: May 24, 2003) Manley Hot Springs POPULATION : 73 (2002 est.) LOCATION : About 5 miles north of the Tanana River on Hot Springs Slough, at the end of the Elliott Highway, 160 road miles west of Fairbanks. DESCRIPTION : A small community whose hot springs have drawn Fairbanks residents and others year-round for 100 years. The local economy is based on a wide variety of small businesses, with many residents having three or four means of producing income. A barter system also thrives. Government employment accounts for about one quarter of the total. Nine residents hold commercial fishing permits. Gardening, hunting and fishing provide food sources. There is one school, attended by about 15 students. HISTORY : In 1902 John Karshner, a mining prospector, discov…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Community Datebook (Published: May 21, 2003) BLOOD BANK Blood Bank of Alaska has ongoing needs. Donations may be made 10 a.m.-6 p.m. Monday through Saturday at 4000 Laurel St. Appointments are recommended at 563-3110 in Anchorage, 1-907-260-5672 in Soldotna and 1-907-376-1195 in Wasilla. Current needs include: Condition Red (one day supply): O-negative. Condition Green (three- or more day supply): O-positive, A-positive, B-positive, B-negative, AB-positive, A-negative, AB-negative. Today SCHOOL LUNCH Anchorage: Elementary school -- Chicken nuggets with Tater Tots or pepperoni pizza or chicken burrito, cinnamon applesauce, Orange Smiles, milk. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Sporting goods technology misses the point (Published: May 16, 2003) Outside magazine is out with its "Outside Buyer's Guide'' this month or, as some call it, "The Buy, Buy, Buy Edition.'' Whatever happened to the idea of people escaping to the outdoors to reconnect with the ancient simplicity of life, to seek escape from the increasingly complex and technologically confounding modern world? Today's Americans take more high technology to the wilderness than the Apollo astronauts took to the moon, and that's no joke. The electronics involved in Apollo were pretty simple. The electronics involved in today's wilderness, or at least Outside's version of it, are incredibly complex. Consider, from the "Adventure Electronics" pages of the "Outside Buyer's Guide":  "Sony's CLIE' Peg-NZ90 PDA …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Outdoors hot line (Published: May 4, 2003) The Alaska Department of Fish and Game has stocked Jewel Lake with rainbow trout, DeLong lake with char, and will soon begin putting trout, char and grayling into another 26 Anchorage area lakes from Eklutna to Portage. Jewel Lake was the first city lake to get stocked this year, and anglers were already catching fish there including three-year-old Brendan Reilly. Fish and Game reminds anglers that the daily limit is five rainbow trout per day and five in possession. To find out more about which lakes will get stocked when, check the Fish and Game fish stocking update at www.sf.adfg.state.ak.us/ statewide/hatchery/Stocking_search/ HTML/stock_search.cfm . Or call Fish and Game at 907-267-2218. (Photo by BOB HALLINEN / Anchorage Daily News ) Cli…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Meetings (Published: May 19, 2003) Monday  Anchorage Chamber of Commerce will meet at noon at the 4th Avenue Theatre. Homeland security is the topic. R.S.V.P. to 272-2401 or www.anchoragechamber.org in the Calendar of Events section. Tuesday  Soldotna Chamber of Commerce will meet at noon at the Riverside House. City Manager Tom Boedeker will talk about the city's budget. For more information, call 1-907-262-9814.  Greater Wasilla Chamber of Commerce will meet at noon at the Mat-Su Resort. Speaker is Tony Izzo, Alaska president of Enstar. Call 1-907-376-1299 for more information. Wednesday  Alaska Chapter of the Project Management Institute meets at 6 p.m. at the Petroleum Club of Anchorage. Guest speakers will be Mark Enzenberger and Charlyne Fair of GCI. R.S.V.P. by today to Jim …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Usibelli plans to build power plant YEARS AWAY: Coal-fired project in Healy would link to existing intertie system. The Associated Press (Published: May 15, 2003) Usibelli Coal Mine Inc. has announced a proposal for a $421 million, 200-megawatt power plant at its mine near Healy. It would be a major energy project designed to supply low-cost electricity to Alaska's "Railbelt" energy grid, a Usibelli official said. Managers of electric utilities greeted the proposal with some caution, however. Steve Haagenson, president and chief executive officer of Golden Valley Electric Association in Fairbanks, said he met with Usibelli in April and reviewed the proposal. He said the plan has merits, but he has some reservations. Steve Denton, vice president of business development for Usibelli, tol…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Art of the real estate deal is in negotiations Chris Stephens real estate (Published: May 11, 2003) One of the most important tasks in commercial real estate is negotiating. Most common are sales, purchases and leases. But as we all know, negotiating applies to just about every agreement whether business or personal. Success starts long before you sit down at the table. Success requires a plan. This was the message I heard recently at a commercial real estate negotiations seminar. The seminar, sponsored by the Anchorage chapter of the Building Owners and Managers Association, stressed the importance of planning for negotiations, which among other things means looking at all your alternatives. The seminar also outlined an easy way to think through the alternatives, which I'll share with…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | ACS goes public with yellow pages OFFERING: Local business raises $125 million on the Toronto Stock Exchange. By RICHARD RICHTMYER Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 9, 2003) Alaska Communications Systems on Thursday spun off its yellow pages business into a separate operating company, raising $125 million in an initial public offering on the Toronto Stock Exchange. Called ACS Media, the new company will continue to be based in Anchorage and customers' accounts will not be affected by the change, the company said. "They're going to see a stronger yellow pages, but aside from that, this is an invisible transaction for customers," said ACS vice president Mary Anne Pease. After subtracting underwriting and other fees, ACS cleared about $118 million on the deal. ACS will retain a 12.6 pe…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Vending machine firm eyes ASI plant FISH OR CHIPS? State listens as Tasty Fries Inc. says it could employ 3,000. By WESLEY LOY Anchorage Daily News (Published: April 30, 2003) A Pennsylvania company with plans to manufacture a patented french fry vending machine is eying the mostly vacant Alaska Seafood International plant near the Anchorage airport as a place to make the machines. Representatives of Tasty Fries Inc. of Blue Bell, Pa., met with Anchorage Mayor George Wuerch in early April, said Kevin Bruce, the city's director of economic development. The Tasty Fries people talked up the virtues of the giant, state-owned factory building and the city's location as a global transportation hub, he said. The major concern about the Tasty Fries idea, however, is money, said Kevin Pearson, …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Law catches up to Alaska fugitive in L.A. BANKING SCANDAL: After nearly a decade on the lam, Miklautsch will stand trial. By RICHARD RICHTMYER Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 21, 2003) Former Alaska businessman Thomas Miklautsch, a fugitive since 1994, has been arrested and will stand trial on bank fraud, conspiracy and other charges in federal court in Anchorage, officials said. Miklautsch was arrested at the Los Angeles International Airport late last month, federal law enforcement officials said. He was prominent in Fairbanks political and business circles, with investments in the North Slope oil fields and other ventures, and he is a former member of the University of Alaska Board of Regents. A magistrate ordered Miklautsch returned to Anchorage, and he is in the custody of fe…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | House extends RCA LIFELINE: Agency receives 4-year extension; big changes in the works. By MARY PEMBERTON The Associated Press (Published: May 20, 2003) JUNEAU -- The House threw a lifeline to the Regulatory Commission of Alaska on Monday, giving the agency that oversees utilities a four-year extension. But not before it ordered the embattled agency to make significant changes in the way it does business. During hours of contentious debate, lawmakers approved legislation that extends the sunset date of the agency that was set to enter a yearlong phase-out beginning June 30. Some lawmakers critical of the agency say it is inhibiting Alaska Communications System Inc. -- the state's largest telephone company -- from competing with General Communications Inc. ACS has a majority of the stat…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Early-morning fire damages downtown hair salon, spa ALLURE DAY: Deputy fire marshal estimates damage at $500,000. By NICOLE TSONG Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 19, 2003) Fire ravaged the interior of Allure Day Spa and Hair Design early Sunday morning, suspending operations of the downtown salon and spa for at least a week, its owners said. A security system alerted owners that there was a problem at the building and they went to check it out, said Cleo Hill, deputy fire marshal. They called 911 at 3:49 a.m. after they saw smoke. By 4:01 a.m., the battalion chief had called in additional units for the fire at 142 W. Fifth Ave. and the blaze was under control by 4:40 a.m., she said. Flames burned through the floor between the first and second stories of the building and burned hot…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Tidal waves of cash transform life in Akhiok OIL SPILL CHECKS: Some residents quickly spend $200,000 from Exxon. By BLAINE HARDEN The Washington Post (Published: May 19, 2003) AKHIOK -- This lonely fishing village is accustomed to torment. Out in the Gulf of Alaska at the southern end of Kodiak Island, it has endured earthquakes, tsunamis, black rains of volcanic ash and, 14 years ago this week, the most environmentally destructive oil spill in history. In recent months, though, Akhiok has been hit by tidal waves -- of money. Checks for $100,000 rolled in last August. Another round of $100,000 payments arrived in January. Most adults here have cashed two lottery-sized checks because they voted overwhelmingly last year to cash out most of a $36 million trust fund owned by the Native vil…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Field Notes (Published: May 18, 2003) Boating Head on down to Goose Lake for Canoe Fun Day The fourth-annual Anchorage Canoe Fun Day is 1 to 4 p.m. today at Goose Lake near UAA. The U.S. Coast Guard Auxiliary, Backcountry Safaris and other sponsors will make sure everyone is outfitted with a boat and paddling gear. This free family-oriented event is designed to introduce canoeing to first-timers, especially children. Activities include a raffle for a handmade wood strip canoe and a boating safety display. (222-1632, www.backcountrysafaris.com/canoeday.shtml ). Birding Head to the zoo today for Migratory Bird Day Today is International Migratory Bird Day at the Alaska Zoo from 11 a.m. to 6 p.m. Come celebrate birds, and the 100th anniversary of the National Wildlife Refuge System. There…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | 2 men ruled guilty in Brown beating death TRIAL: Grossman, David convicted of second-degree murder in 2001 fight. By SHEILA TOOMEY Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 16, 2003) It took jurors less than a day to find William "Donnie" Grossman and Erick David guilty of second-degree murder in the beating death two summers ago of Larry Brown. The jury Thursday also found David guilty of misdemeanor assault on another man, Kevin Vanderway, who was in a vacant lot in Fairview on Aug. 14, 2001, when a fight broke out. Jurors rejected a whole range of lesser verdicts available to them and convicted the two men as charged. "I'm thrilled that they were able to hold these guys accountable," prosecutor Adrienne Bachman said after the jury went home and the defendants went back to jail. The victi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Price-fixing trial could go to jury Friday SALMON SUIT: Lawyers say collusion cheated fishermen. By WESLEY LOY Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 21, 2003) After nearly four months of argument, attorneys in the Bristol Bay salmon price-fixing trial began closing statements Tuesday in Superior Court in Anchorage. Jurors could begin deliberations as soon as Friday in the civil case in which a class of about 4,500 commercial fishermen accuse salmon processors and Japanese seafood importers of illegally conspiring to suppress prices for sockeye salmon. Defense lawyers for those companies deny the accusation. Parker Folse, one of the fishermen's attorneys, on Friday told the panel of 12 jurors and six alternates that the companies willfully worked together to keep prices low. He said the …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Inside Alaska business (Published: May 20, 2003) SARS site offered for travelers The Centers for Disease Control recently issued guidance to people traveling from areas affected by SARS. "Interim Guidance for Institutions or Organizations Hosting Persons Arriving in the United States from Areas with Severe Acute Respiratory Syndrome" is available at www.cdc.gov/ncidod/sars/hostingarrivals.htm . "Interim Guidelines for Businesses and Other Organizations with Employees Returning to the United States from Areas with SARS" can be found at www.cdc.gov/ncidod/sars/business_guidelines.htm . Alaska 8(a) conference scheduled The Alaska 8(a) Association is holding a one-day conference in Anchorage on May 28 titled "Issues in 8(a) Contracting." 8(a) refers to a government set-aside program for sm…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | House measure repeals Chitina dipnet fee DISPUTE: Most of the money now goes to Native corporations. The Associated Press (Published: May 14, 2003) JUNEAU -- Dipnetting at Chitina shouldn't mean a dip into your wallet, the state House has concluded. The House voted 31-4 Monday to repeal the $25 fee to dipnet in the Copper River at Chitina. The bill now moves to the Senate. Most of the money raised by the Chitina fee goes to the Chitina and Ahtna Native corporations, which have been involved in a long-running dispute over whether Copper River dipnetters trespass on their land. In 1992, the Alaska Supreme Court ruled that users had access to the river through a 300-foot-wide state right of way. But with no official survey to delineate exactly where the public has access, the state has be…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Senate changes No. 1 use for state forests to timber The Associated Press (Published: May 14, 2003) JUNEAU -- The state Senate has approved a bill that would change the primary purpose of state forests from multi-use to timber harvesting. The bill passed 12-8 Monday, with Republicans in support and Democrats opposing and no debate on the Senate floor. It now heads to the House. Supporters have pitched the bill as a way to promote the timber industry and resource development. "Hopefully, with this legislation, and with the encouragement of this administration, we can see significant additional utilization of a terribly underutilized resource," said Sen. Robin Taylor, R-Wrangell, the sponsor of the bill. Opponents say the changes would lessen oversight and public input into forest manage…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Ram's death puzzles hikers, game experts WHY? Experienced trekkers find full-curl sheep weak and dying. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 16, 2003) Over the 30 years of climbing that took Jim Sayler to the summits of the 230 tallest peaks in Chugach State Park, he had never seen anything like this. Coming down from Rainbow Peak, 18 miles south of Anchorage, with his wife, Kyle, Sayler almost stepped on a mature, big-horned Dall sheep ram. Sayler had been close to plenty of sheep before. One can hardly hike the state park at Anchorage's back door without seeing sheep. But he had never been this close. Then the late-April encounter grew truly unusual. "The dog was barking,'' Sayler said, "and we're just sitting there, oblivious, just off the trail. It was obvious he (t…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Begich salutes growth consortium VISION ANCHORAGE: Mayor-elect used group's plan as model for economic development ideas. By RICHARD RICHTMYER Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 14, 2003) Mayor-elect Mark Begich pledged support Tuesday to an economic development plan during the Vision Anchorage forum at the Sheraton Hotel. Vision Anchorage chairman Rick Morrison is at right. (Photo by Jim Lavrakas / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Mayor-elect Mark Begich on Tuesday pledged his support for an economic development plan championed by a consortium of growth, development and civic groups, saying that he used it as a model for his campaign's economic development proposals. The consortium, called Vision Anchorage, has been working since 2001 to develop a collaborative econom…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Business Meetings (Published: May 12, 2003) Today  Anchorage Chamber of Commerce meets at noon at the 4th Avenue Theatre for the 2003 STAR Awards recognizing successful school-business partnerships. R.S.V.P. to 272-2401 or www.anchoragechamber.org in the Calendar of Events section. Tuesday  Vision Anchorage rolls out the blueprints to Anchorage's economic future at a forum at the Sheraton Anchorage Hotel from noon to 1:30 p.m. The forum will address topics such as creating an educated work force, the city's ability to attract new businesses and a quality of life through a healthy economy. Speakers will be Rick Morrison, Vision Anchorage chairman; Mayor-elect Mark Begich; and leaders from Vision Anchorage participating organizations including the Anchorage and Chugiak-Eagle River cham…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Satellite service up in the air TELEVISION: Dish Network ready to broadcast local stations, but they say no deal is near. By RICHARD RICHTMYER Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 7, 2003) Satellite television provider Dish Network says it's ready to beam local Anchorage stations into homes across Alaska, but the managers of some of those stations say they're nowhere close to an agreement that would allow them to do so. EchoStar Communications, Dish Network's owner, has been trying in earnest to add local channels to its lineup in Alaska since February 2002, when it launched two new satellites into an orbit that gives them a better angle at Alaska. The new satellites allow a stronger signal and more stations. They also allow local customers to use smaller satellite dishes. Lack of loca…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | PR firm hired to polish city's image CONTRACT: DCI to lure businesses to 'the middle of everywhere.' By RICHARD RICHTMYER Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 3, 2003) Anchorage has an image problem, and city boosters have turned to an East Coast public relations firm to help solve it. The Anchorage Economic Development Corp. has signed a three-year, $450,000 contract with Development Counsellors International, a New York City-based firm that specializes in winning national and international media exposure for cities, states and foreign countries. Its mission: to counter the misperceptions AEDC officials say many Outside business people have about Alaska and persuade them to move to Anchorage. Kevin Pearson, the AEDC's vice president of business development, said many corporate executi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Education secretary gets eyeful TOUR: Rod Paige sees firsthand the various challenges facing the Bush. By DAN JOLING The Associated Press (Published: May 6, 2003) Education Secretary Rod Paige said it is too soon to consider exempting Alaska from federal law. (Photo by Al Grillo / The Associated Press ) Click on photo to enlarge TUNTUTULIAK -- U.S. Secretary of Education Rod Paige was not ready to grant Alaska exceptions to sweeping federal education reforms after visiting a rural village Monday, but he did have an assessment of its educators. "Heroic teachers," Paige said after a little more than two hours at Lewis Angapak Memorial School in Tuntutuliak. "What it does is move you to do all you can to be helpful." Paige arrived Saturday at the invitation of Sens. Ted Stevens and Lisa M…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Former Alaska Pulp workers win back pay settlement The Associated Press SITKA (May 14, 4:00 p.m. ADT) - Former employees of the Alaska Pulp Corporation mill have won a settlement for back pay and benefits worth almost $12 million dollars. The dispute between workers and APC began in 1987 and has outlasted even the mill itself, which closed in 1993. The National Labor Relations board announced the end of the 16-year dispute earlier this week between the company and 95 workers who went on strike for ten months in 1986. The labor board had ruled in 1989 that the company was liable for back pay and retirement benefits and that the company had violated the National Labor Relations act when, after the strike, it failed to offer qualified strikers fair job reinstatement. The board found that …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Feds OK halibut subsistence fishery TRADITION: Regulations will allow 117 communities and 120 tribal groups to participate. The Associated Press (Published: May 5, 2003) JUNEAU -- Residents of rural communities and Alaska Native groups with a tradition of subsistence fishing will be allowed to catch 20 halibut a day under new federal halibut subsistence fishing regulations that takes effect May 15. The regulations are an attempt to codify what many Alaskans were doing already: fishing halibut for personal use and in some cases exceeding the personal-use catch limit of two a day. Alaska does have regulated subsistence fisheries, mainly for salmon, but halibut has never been included. "The regulations ... were advocated by people from smaller communities who were engaged in subsistence f…

summary: | | Wrist computer is a winner in user-friendly category X6 HR: Device packs a lot into small package and leaves competitors behind. By CRAIG MEDRED Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 11, 2003) The new Suunto X6 HR is a watch, chronometer, altimeter, electronic compass and heart-rate monitor that is easy to use. (Photo by Evan R. Steinhauser / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge Techno-worshipping gearheads will have a hard time avoiding a love affair with Suunto's new X6 HR wrist computer. This Finnish company's second attempt to marry a watch/compass/altimeter with a heart-rate monitor offers the one feature the first effort sorely lacked -- user friendliness. Just imagine, a functional piece of electronic wizardry you can figure out without consulting a mind-boggling manua…

summary: | | Kids learn about respect, responsibility, choices EAGLE RIVER: New program got students to help whole community. By ROSE COX Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 4, 2003) Participants in the Respect and Responsibility program at Ravenwood Elementary School in Eagle River initiated projects that benefited Anchorage and Eagle River communities. Ambassadors wear donated shades. (Photo by Bill Roth / Anchorage Daily News) Click on photo to enlarge An idea conceived by Karen Kirk while she was pursuing her master's degree took root and blossomed at an Eagle River elementary school this year. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Talent search benefits Wish Upon the North Star FUND-RAISER: Categories include vocal, dance and an open competition. By ROSE COX Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 3, 2003) An upcoming fund-raiser for a local group that grants wishes to seriously ill children might make others' dreams come true too. The junior and adult winners in the Wish Upon the North Star Talent Search will each receive an all-expenses paid trip to their choice of Burbank, Calif., or Los Angeles. They may also get a chance to appear on either CBS's Star Search or the Jay Leno show. Alaska Capital Entertainment, which is organizing the competition, will facilitate and pay for a tape of the winners to be made for submission to the shows. The event is sponsored by Anchorage National Association for the Advancement of Colored People. title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Governor: Legislature's budgets fall short 'WORK TO DO': Murkowski praises effort, wants targets met. By MIKE CHAMBERS The Associated Press (Published: May 3, 2003) JUNEAU -- House and Senate leaders need to find $100 million more in either budget cuts or new revenues, Gov. Frank Murkowski said Friday. The Republican governor met with GOP legislative leaders Friday after the Senate approved its version of the operating budget for fiscal 2004 earlier in the week. The House had already approved its version of the budget. Now the two versions will go to a conference committee to find compromises. Murkowski said lawmakers will have to come up with a plan that meets his "budget targets." "We've got a lot of work to do over the next few weeks," said Sen. Gary Wilken, R-Fairbanks. Wilken is c…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Digest (Published: May 15, 2003) Cody Williams, 17, sits at his home in Kasilof on Tuesday as his mother replaces bandages on the injuries he suffered during a bear attack Sunday. The teenager is recovering from a broken hand, puncture wounds and gashes after being charged by a brown bear while hunting. (Photo by M. SCOTT MOON / Associated Press) Click on photo to enlarge ANCHORAGE Smoking woman severely burned after catching nightgown on fire A 58-year-old woman caught her nightgown on fire while smoking Tuesday evening and suffered severe burns over half her body, fire officials said. The Anchorage Fire Department was called to 5331 E. 26th Ave. just after 6 p.m., after a report of a female complaining of severe burns, said AFD spokesman Tom Kempton. The woman was found sittin…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Field Notes (Published: May 11, 2003) BIRDING Migratory bird day set for next weekend International Migratory Bird Day is coming up next Sunday, May 18, from 11 a.m. to 6 p.m. at the Alaska Zoo. Zoo admission will be half price and a shuttle bus will run every 15 minutes from Service High School. Events include storytelling, raptor presentations, kids' activities and crafts, live music and a bald eagle release at 6:15 p.m. at O'Malley's on the Green, 3651 O'Malley Road (a shuttle will be available from the zoo). (Alaska Zoo, 346-3242). BOATING Join sailing club for work party, potluck The Alaska Sailing Club's "Get Ready for Fun" work party is noon to 6 p.m. Saturday. Club members will gather at the club at Mile 8.3 of South Big Lake Road to build a floating dock, repair other docks, c…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Native-hire preference not lawful RULING: Alaska Supreme Court deals loss to North Slope Borough. By SHEILA TOOMEY Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 17, 2003) The Alaska Supreme Court ruled unanimously Friday that a North Slope Borough Native-hire preference is unconstitutional. In an opinion written by Justice Alex Bryner, the court said a law requiring that even unqualified Native applicants for borough jobs be hired ahead of qualified non-Natives violates the promise in the Alaska Constitution of equal protection to all Alaskans regardless of race or national origin. It is possible that some governmental interest might be so important that it outweighs some of these rights some of the time, the court said. But the borough's desire to improve employment opportunities for its Inupi…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Knowles meets with top Democrats on D.C. trip CAPITOL HILL: Former governor met with Senate leader. By LIZ RUSKIN Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 7, 2003) WASHINGTON -- Former Gov. Tony Knowles, long rumored to be a candidate for the U.S. Senate, made a quiet visit to Washington this week. He and his wife, Susan Knowles, were spotted in the halls of the Senate on Tuesday by Capitol Hill aides and political operatives. Among others, Knowles met with Senate Minority Leader Tom Daschle, D-S.D., and Sen. Evan Bayh, D-Ind., their spokesmen confirmed. Efforts to contact the former governor Tuesday were unsuccessful. Knowles, a Democrat, reached the end of the two-term limit and left office in December, when former U.S. Sen. Frank Murkowski was sworn in. If Knowles does run, he would be …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Digest (Published: May 9, 2003) KETCHIKAN SARS isolation procedures used, but patients didn't have virus Ketchikan General Hospital tested its SARS isolation procedures after two patients showed some SARS virus-like symptoms. Severe acute resperatory syndrome was later ruled out as a diagnosis by a local doctor in conjunction with the Alaska Section of Epidemiology, according to hospital officials. The patients were released. The two individuals, identified as visitors to Ketchikan, arrived at the hospital emergency room early Tuesday morning. They displayed some of the symptoms of SARS, so the hospital immediately began its new isolation procedures, officials said. The procedures are part of a SARS policy developed by the hospital, local clinics, the Alaska Public Health Depart…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Two fathers share miserable day during courtroom hearing MURDER: Texas man can't take son home while he awaits trial, judge says. By SHEILA TOOMEY Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 14, 2003) Two fathers showed up for a court hearing Tuesday afternoon and listened to lawyerly debate about the ruined lives of their sons. Ted Dirkes' son, Keith, is dead. He was an 18-year-old college student working as night manager of a Godfather's Pizza on Sept. 25, 1999, when an armed robber put two bullets in his head. That was more than three years ago, and the man accused of executing Keith Dirkes has not yet been tried. Ted Dirkes is frustrated beyond words, but he keeps showing up. He has no role in the debate about how the prosecution or the defense is handled. No one asks him to say anything.…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Bill would redirect oil funds RAID? Revenues would go to state rather than Permanent Fund principal. By MIKE CHAMBERS The Associated Press (Published: May 13, 2003) (Jim Lavrakas) Click on photo to enlarge JUNEAU -- A bill to redirect back into state coffers millions in oil revenues bound for the Permanent Fund was approved Monday by the Senate. And Gov. Frank Murkowski said Monday he supports the bill, increasing the likelihood that it will finally pass in the Legislature after being rejected in previous years. The measure rolls back a decision by the Legislature in 1980 to increase the share of oil revenues going into the principal of the Permanent Fund. At that time, the state was flush with oil money and the Legislature voted to put 50 percent of royalty revenue from post-1980 fiel…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Believe pain when fish say, 'ouch' (Published: May 11, 2003) There's a whale of an international debate underway about whether fish feel pain. First, U.S. scientist James D. Rose weighed in with the opinion they don't. The University of Wyoming professor reported in "Reviews of Fisheries Science'' that fish lack the nerve for it, so to speak. Specifically, Rose said, the primitive brains of fish never developed the areas of the cerebral cortex that enable higher vertebrates to experience pain. Fish respond to stimulus all right, he said, but there is a distinct difference between this response and feeling pain. "Pain is predicated on awareness," Rose said. "The key issue is the distinction between nociception and pain. A person who is anesthetized in an operating theatre will still res…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Businesses, individuals can recycle electronics gear HAZARDOUS: Alaska has highest per capita use of computers, which have lead. Daily News staff (Published: May 8, 2003) Other cities are well ahead of Anchorage when it comes to recycling old computers, VCRs, televisions and other electronic gear. But catching up ought to be easy, and it will start this weekend when the city holds its first electronics recycling event, according to Green Star, the nonprofit group that promotes waste reduction, energy conservation and pollution prevention among local businesses. "We're a little behind the ball when it comes to electronics recycling," said Jeanne Carlson, Green Star's program coordinator. "That's why we think we're going to get a lot of stuff." Alaska has the highest per capita use of co…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Teachers learn about 'Assets' at program YOUTHS: Safe & Drug Free Schools Fair looks at development. By S. JANE SZABO Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 2, 2003) In packets distributed at the fifth annual Safe & Drug Free Schools Fair on April 17, educators found booklets on "40 Ways Anyone Can Build Assets," "150 Ways to Show Kids You Care" and "10 Things Parents Need to Know About Their Teens." But it all boiled down to one thing for Clarissa Smith, a 4-H program staff member seated at one of the large round tables at the Hotel Captain Cook. "My husband and I decided that it's a lot easier to build a child than repair an adult," she explained about the philosophy that has sustained her and her husband, Brian, through raising seven children. Caring is at the heart of building a chil…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Audit rips DFYS kids' travel costs FULL FARES: Planning could save state $650,000 a year, auditors say. By LISA DEMER Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 5, 2003) The state is wasting hundreds of thousands of dollars a year by paying full fare for plane tickets for children under state protection who need to travel, a new state audit says. Often, top-dollar tickets are the only ones available by the time all the required approvals within the Division of Family and Youth Services are met, according to the legislative audit, dated March 21 and released last week. "With a little advance planning, you can make it so much cheaper," said Pat Davidson, legislative auditor. The state covers travel costs for children in DFYS custody. That includes trips to move to a new foster or adoptive home…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Massachusetts legislator defends ANWR wilderness bill The Associated Press FAIRBANKS (May 2, 10:40 a.m. ADT) - U.S. Rep. Ed Markey, a Massachusetts Democrat, said people would not be prohibited from using snowmachines under his bill to make the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge's coastal plain an official wilderness area. Markey was disputing a claim by Rep. Richard Pombo and Gov. Frank Murkowski at a hearing in Kaktovik in April that Markey's bill could block the kinds of mechanized travel that are allowed in Alaska's current wilderness areas. Markey discussed the matter Thursday in a letter to Pombo, R-Calif., chairman of the House Resources Committee. Israel Klein, a spokesman for Markey, said the transcripts of the Kaktovik hearing show the bill was "clearly mischaracterized as preve…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaska Supreme Court won't hear Tok wolf trapping case LAST HURRAH: Friends of Animals will have to pay about $120,000. The Associated Press (Published: May 1, 2003) FAIRBANKS -- The Alaska Supreme Court has refused to consider an appeal of the nearly $200,000 damage award against an animal rights group whose Alaska researcher cut an injured wolf out of a Tok man's trap back in 1997. Wildlife biologist Gordon Haber and Friends of Animals, which supported his work, were successfully sued by Tok trapper Eugene Johnson over the freeing of an injured wolf from his trapline in 1997. In an order released Friday, the justices announced that they would not take up the case. The Supreme Court had previously issued an order refusing to hear an appeal from Haber, who was also successfully sued. T…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Federal money will go toward raptor center BIRDS: Sen. Ted Stevens wrangles $350,000 for clinic and nature education center. The Associated Press (Published: April 7, 2003) JUNEAU-- Federal money secured by Sen. Ted Stevens will go toward helping injured eagles, owls and ravens at a new perch near the Brotherhood Bridge. The $350,000 will enable the city to purchase 10.5 acres of private land in the Mendenhall Valley. The property, which is near the Kaxhdighoowu Heen Trail and the Mendenhall River, will be used for a bird rehabilitation clinic and nature education center. The Juneau Raptor Center, the Trust for Public Land and the city have been working with Stevens' office on the acquisition for three years. The land offers a permanent place to treat injured birds and educate the publ…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Picking an Anchorage mayor is rarely easy HISTORY: Five of the city's first eight mayoral elections have ended in runoffs. By DON HUNTER Anchorage Daily News (Published: May 1, 2003) Think figuring out who won the mayor's race this year was hard? It has never been easy in Anchorage. Even in the days when a candidate needed only a shade more than 40 percent of the votes to take office, the outcome more often than not was decided in a runoff. Campaign 2003 hit an especially tricky chuckhole. At the same time voters were picking a mayor on April 1, they also changed the percentage of votes the winner needed. The change allowed Mark Begich to claim a victory -- after a recount but without a runoff. That's unusual in Anchorage politics. When voters unified the old city and borough governmen…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | South Seas field trip no picnic for Alaska scientists STUDY: Experts endure adversity studying rare birds in winter habitat. By DOUG O'HARRA Anchorage Daily News (Published: April 28, 2003) Blazing sun, crashing surf and exotic shorebirds on the wing. Days spent hiking white-sand beaches of deserted Polynesian atolls, partly for a chance to see Alaska's rare bristle-thighed curlew in its winter home. Does this sound like an idyllic spring break or what? Not quite. During a three-week survey last month to islets scattered across about 500 miles of the Tuamotu Archipelago southeast of Tahiti, three Anchorage scientists found themselves seasick, sunburned, scratched from coral, dodging little sharks and exhausted after daylong marches through 100-degree heat. "It was far harder field work…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Domenici bides time on pushing ANWR drilling ANOTHER DAY: Too much opposition right now, senator says. By H. JOSEF HEBERT The Associated Press (Published: April 7, 2003) WASHINGTON -- There will be no attempt to revive drilling in the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge as part of a Senate energy bill, says the Republican senator who will guide the legislation. Sen. Pete Domenici, R-N.M., said the Senate's recent rejection of a proposal to develop oil in the refuge has made it clear that another such confrontation will only fail and could jeopardize broader energy legislation. "There's nothing mysterious about it. We'll lose. We'll bring it up, debate it for an hour or two. We'll vote (and) lose," said Domenici in an interview, explaining why he has no intention of pursuing the issue, thou…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Meetings draw big parks plans IDEAS: Residents gather to shape future of city's park system. By ROSEMARY SHINOHARA Anchorage Daily News (Published: April 14, 2003) The Campbell Creek greenbelt should be connected to Kincaid Park, Anchorage should set aside space where dogs can run free, and the city needs more parks staffers and better maintenance. Residents who participated in town meetings last week to help shape the city's future parks and open space plan came up with these ideas and more, to improve what many agree is an enviable parks system. "We want those signs that say 'All dogs must be leashed' gone" in some designated places, said Weimaraner owner Cindy Williams, among about 75 people brainstorming during a meeting Thursday night at Scenic Park Elementary School. The city has…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | McGrath wolf kills fall short EXPERIMENT: Murkowski left control up to trappers; state will still relocate bears. By JOEL GAY Anchorage Daily News (Published: April 25, 2003) Trappers killed 15 wolves around McGrath this winter, slightly more than average but too few to accomplish the goal of an experimental predator control plan. The Alaska Department of Fish and Game had proposed to eliminate all predators from a 520-square-mile experimental micromanagement area for three years. The plan called for killing 45 to 48 wolves and temporarily relocating brown and black bears during the spring calving season. Biologists say such efforts should let the moose population rebound and provide a higher harvest for local hunters. But the experiment appears to have been undercut by Gov. Frank Murk…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | State considers how to use salmon funds EXTRA: About $13 million remains from a 3-year federal grant. The Associated Press (Published: April 14, 2003) JUNEAU -- The state is trying to determine how to use millions of dollars earmarked to help Southeast's salmon industry. About $13 million remains in the state's Southeast Sustainable Salmon Fund from a $60 million, three-year grant from the federal government. The money will be divided among four categories: infrastructure, marketing, fish stock enhancement and education, said Ken Alper, a planner with the state Department of Fish and Game. The money came from the federal Pacific Coast Salmon Recovery Fund and was disbursed over three years to Alaska, Washington, Oregon, California and the Columbia River Tribes. The state has contracted…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Governor's spending plan grows in House BUDGET: Legislators want more for secondary schools, less for university, than Murkowski. By MIKE CHAMBERS The Associated Press (Published: April 9, 2003) JUNEAU -- The House is poised to approve a fiscal 2004 spending plan that restores more than $50 million in cuts proposed by Gov. Frank Murkowski. House Republicans met for several hours behind closed doors on Tuesday to hash out an agreement among members of their caucus on a final plan to send to the Senate. The measure includes about $20 million more for K-12 schools than was first proposed by Murkowski, and a general fund budget for the University of Alaska that provides no increase over the current year's total. House Majority Leader John Coghill, R-North Pole, said much of the discussion …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Plan would allow transfer of bycatch Laine Welch Fisheries (Published: April 12, 2003) KODIAK -- In a concept similar to swapping industrial pollution credits, fishery managers are considering a plan that allows transfers of halibut accidentally caught, called bycatch, between trawl fisheries. "If they are able to prosecute a fishery in a manner that produces less bycatch, the trawlers would like to take and apply that bycatch to fisheries they currently can't prosecute because they've reached the caps," said Stosh Anderson, a member of the North Pacific Fishery Management Council. The concept of bycatch transfers is drawing boos from some. "Any new plan should advance conservation in a way that has not been achieved in the current open access fishery. The way the plan is being designe…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | War protesters in Soldotna doused COLD WATER: Man whose son is a Marine in Iraq is cited by police in incident. By TOM KIZZIA Anchorage Daily News (Published: April 9, 2003) Several anti-war protesters, including a middle-aged Quaker woman holding a sign saying "Consider the Children," were doused with a bucket of cold water at Soldotna's main intersection last week by a man standing in the back of a passing pickup truck. Soldotna fishing guide Jeff Webster, who has a son in the Marines in Iraq, was tracked down by Soldotna police and cited for harassment, a misdemeanor. A week earlier, he had done the same thing to two women holding signs at the same corner. At first the protesters turned the other cheek, despite freezing temperatures. "We said pressing charges didn't seem in keeping …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Alaskans to test Stryker vehicles ARMY: Light armored vehicle to take service in new direction, colonel says. By BRUCE SCHREINER The Associated Press (Published: April 17, 2003) FORT KNOX, Ky. -- A new generation of light armored vehicles will soon be rumbling across Fort Knox's rolling terrain, some piloted by Alaska soldiers, as the Army transforms itself into a more mobile fighting force. Hundreds of soldiers -- mainly from Fort Drum, N.Y.; Fort Lewis, Wash.; and Fort Richardson -- this summer will test the Stryker, an armored troop carrier that comes equipped with high-powered technology to enable commanders to scan the battlefield. "It's a transformation vehicle," Army Col. Philip North said at a press conference Wednesday. "It's the way the Army is going right now. We are moving …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: | | Initiative aims to tax cruise ship industry PROPOSAL: Citizens action would impose $46 levy on each passenger. By CATHY BROWN The Associated Press (Published: April 30, 2003) JUNEAU -- A citizens initiative to tax cruise ships and require them to get environmental permits was filed Tuesday with the lieutenant governor's office. Joe Geldhof, a Juneau attorney who is a sponsor of the measure, said the initiative would impose a $46 tax on each cruise ship passenger. It also would subject the industry to the state corporate income tax and impose a 33 percent tax on shipboard gambling income. The initiative would also require the ships to get permits before they can discharge waste in Alaska waters. It would require independent observers aboard the ships to monitor pollution control, sanita…
